text: Tier system adds complexity to solve a problem (heaviness) that lacks evidence — only 2 debates on record, both completed quickly
Quick tier removes self-review and critique log, breaking Step 10 index tracking and Step 11 completeness verification
Quick tier fragments the dataset, making future historical learning (Idea #8) harder rather than easier
Rejecting role inversion is conjecture, not evidence — self-review catch rates don't speak to role inversion quality
Phasing order is backwards — front-loads the biggest, least-validated change instead of starting with narrow, evidence-producing fixes
Type-specific prompting risks anchoring Codex away from cross-cutting issues
Dynamic round scoring is gameable — atomizing issues or restating in different language to justify continuation
Deep tier creates expectation gradient making Standard feel second-rate
SKILL.md size concern should focus on branching factor and conditional complexity, not raw line count
Deep tier context dilution — later rounds become context-management exercises rather than better critique
Step 6 symmetry problem — if Round 1 becomes type-aware but Round 2 stays generic, domain-specific probing drops off
Severity calibration still lacks a semantic target — does severity measure the flaw, the consequence of ignoring it, or the reversibility?
Mixed-type debates need a composition rule for combined prompts, not just 'additive' handwaving
Prompt/checklist drift is now an immediate implementation problem since type-specific prompts are adopt-first
